title: Choosing a Scheduler
concept: Choosing the right learning rate scheduler depends on your architecture and training budget. Cosine Annealing with Warmup is the modern default, while Step Decay remains a simple, reliable benchmark.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What shape does the learning rate curve follow in Cosine Annealing?

options.text: A smooth, wave-like cosine decay from the peak rate down to near zero
A series of sharp, flat steps dropping 10x at a time
A straight vertical line that spikes instantly

exercises.explain: Yes! A smooth cosine wave shape.

cards.title: Selecting Your Strategy
cards.body: We've covered static rates, step decay, cosine annealing, and warmup. When building a new vision pipeline, how do you choose? Should you build a complex cosine curve or keep it simple with steps? Let's map out the decision.

cards.title: Scheduler Selection Matrix
cards.body:
- Step Decay: Best for simple models and quick prototyping. Easy to set up and debug.
- Cosine Annealing + Warmup: Modern standard. Best for state-of-the-art accuracy, large CNNs, and Transformers.
- Reduce on Plateau: Decays only when validation loss stops dropping. Great for custom datasets.

cards.title: Manual vs. Automatic transmission
cards.body: Step decay is like a manual car: you decide exactly when to shift gears (epochs 30, 60, 90). Cosine annealing is like a smooth automatic transmission: it shifts gears continuously and imperceptibly, keeping the engine running at optimal efficiency without your input.

cards.title: MobileNet Training
cards.body: When training MobileNet, a smooth cosine schedule ensures the compact model converges tightly, squeezing out every fraction of accuracy without needing manual step adjustments.

cards.title: Plateau decay needs patience
cards.body: Reduce on Plateau is very popular because it adapts to validation progress. However, you must configure a 'patience' parameter (how many epochs to wait before decaying). If patience is too short, the scheduler will decay the rate on a temporary flat spot, freezing learning too early.

left.text:
Simple CNN prototypes
Custom datasets with unknown epochs
Easy to debug stepped drops

right.text:
State-of-the-art target accuracy
Deep backbones (ResNet, EfficientNet)
Standard in modern publications

exercises.prompt: Which scheduler decays the learning rate only when the validation loss stops improving for a set number of epochs?
exercises.explain: Correct! Reduce on Plateau monitors validation loss, decaying when learning stalls.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Cosine Annealing with Warmup is considered the modern industry default for training deep CNN architectures.
exercises.explain: True. It is the modern default for training deep backbones and transformers successfully.

exercises.sentence: The 'patience' setting in Reduce on Plateau determines how many epochs to wait before ___ the learning rate.
exercises.explain: Patience is the cooldown epoch count before decaying the rate.

exercises.prompt: What is a main advantage of using Step Decay for a quick CNN prototype?

options.text: It is simple to implement and the stepped drops are highly visible and easy to debug
It completely removes the need for validation data
It automatically generates synthetic training images

exercises.explain: Correct! Step decay is simple and provides clear stepped drops for easy debugging.

exercises.prompt: We have completed our study of learning rate finders, loss plots, and scheduling strategies. What is the next and final unit in Phase 5?

options.text: Test-Time Augmentation (TTA), where we use augmentation during inference to boost final prediction accuracy
Deleting all convolutional kernels and training a dense classifier
Skip evaluation and deploy the model immediately
Converting all weights back to random numbers

exercises.explain: Exactly! Now that we have optimized training, we can explore Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) to boost evaluation accuracy. Next up: Test-Time Augmentation!
